quotedTweet.text:
My house was built on a foundation of wood. It works well normally because I put a whole lot of wood under it. 

But termites came and started to eat the wood. Slowly but surely, the wood got thinned out. My house still was fine. Everybody was still living inside comfortably. I ignored the termites since nobody was complaining and didn't add more wood back. The new narrative was a glut of wood anyway, don't worry.

Suddenly, a big storm came. My shaky wooden foundation crumbled. All hell broke loose. But my neighbour next door with cement foundation was fine. 

How I wish I fixed that termite issue when I had the time.

IYKYK.

#oott

"Any such activity would expose the violating vessels to sanctions. Furthermore, they may be subject to targeting.”

text: The UK has appointed its 13th energy secretary in 20 years (@Miatsf replacing @Ed_Miliband). On average, they have lasted ~18 months on the job. Anywhere else, that would be seen as a bit of a problem.

Ten days into the new phase of the war (which the US said would degrade Iranian attack capabilities in the Strait of Hormuz), the IRGC keeps hitting oil tankers. Overnight, another vessel was hit and the crew had to jump into the lifeboats.

quotedTweet.text: Another ugly night in the Strait of Hormuz, with at least one tanker hit and on fire (according to @UK_MTO warning). The Greek shipowners (plus a few others) are still crossing the strait, at great risk for crews and ships.

text:
Retail traders will lose money in 24/7 oil markets

Most professional traders are unenthusiastic about plans to move to continuous oil trading, including weekends, with new micro futures products aimed at retail customers, but the change is probably inevitable. 

“When it comes to the question of whether the oil industry needs 24/7 trading, my answer is simple: “Hell no.” according to Bloomberg’s chief commodities columnist Javier Blas. 

“From what I hear in the oil-trading community, most share my views. Yet, round-the-clock trading is coming. In fact, it’s essentially already here, via unregulated financial markets,” he wrote. 

Start-up markets have already begun to offer continuous or near-continuous trading in crude oil prices tailored to the needs and financial means of retail customers. 

In response, the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) has requested permission from U.S. regulators to launch its own micro futures contract for 10 barrels rather than the usual 1,000 barrels of crude.  

“Trade oil in your size, on your time, 24/7” the exchange’s website says, enabling customers to sign up for updates about trading ten barrel WTI oil futures. 

Traders as well as analysts and journalists are unlikely to be thrilled at the prospect of continuous markets — meaning more working hours including weekends. 

But technology changes and the commercial logic makes longer trading hours inevitable, as the Bloomberg article makes clear. 

In the long term, most retail traders will lose money because they under-estimate the information and technology advantages held by professional traders and over-estimate their own trading “edge”. 

Longer trading hours, smaller contract sizes and more retail trading are likely to accelerate the wealth transfer from retail “outsiders” to professional “insiders” ...

The full article is free to read on my website: https://t.co/5e4VlSwEki

text: US  general Caine on Iran: war has cost $37.5 billion so far

President Donald Trump is poised to impose fresh levies on products from dozens of economies by Friday, according to people familiar with the matter, a move to ensure his tariff regime remains intact even after stopgap 10% global duties lapse.

The Trump administration last month proposed new tariffs of at least 10% on 60 trading partners, citing what it said were lax forced-labor standards. The president’s team is preparing to impose duties by the week’s end, though it’s not clear if they’ll diverge from the initial proposal, said the people, who requested anonymity to discuss the plans before they’re public.

New Prime Minister Andy Burnham has approved the use of British military bases by the US for what the UK calls defensive strikes against Iran, in a continuation of his predecessor Keir Starmer’s policy even as President Donald Trump ramps up his war.

Starmer led a meeting of senior ministers and officials on Friday to discuss British policy following the resumption of US operations earlier this month, according to people familiar with the matter.

In America’s EV stronghold, another type of vehicle is rapidly gaining ground.

Hybrids accounted for almost one in every four automobiles registered in California in the second quarter, according to newly released data from the California New Car Dealers Association. That was up from a year ago and easily outpaced sales of electric vehicles, which made up 17.8% of the quarter’s total.

Soaring demand for hybrid cars, which offer both a gas tank and electric motor to improve fuel efficiency, is putting them on pace to surpass annual EV sales in the state for the first time since 2020.

text:
STS ops were still showing up in Fujairah waters on the 21st.

Hard to tell if this is dark transit managing to sneak past or just oil that managed to slip out before the conflict escalated, but at the very least, it tells us the flow hasn't hit a total brick wall yet.

Though with Abu Dhabi paring down Aug–Sept tender allocations and squeezing discount diffs, it looks like even the STS run rates are taking a hit.

#oott #iran

Fresh optical satellite print of the CPC terminal from 5 hours ago. And i was running SAR satellite passes on it all weekend. 

We got lucky with back to back orbits over the CPC terminal across the 18th, 19th, and 20th.

Both outer SPMs have been sitting totally bone dry since at least the 19th, with a lone vessel berthed at the center SPM. Still no confirmation on whether that vessel is taking on barrels or just parking there.

What's locked in, though, is that two SPMs have printed zero tanker traffic for three days straight. Safe to say at least two of those SPMs are knocked out of service.

#oott #russia

text:
Key takeaways showing just how choked up the refined products mkt actually is.

It’s scary to think where the tape would be without PADD 3 pulling the weight. Honestly these wild crack spreads aren't even that shocking.

If Trump is going to pull off a TACO, he better move fast. There's still a solid month left of peak summer demand, and US product stocks are running ridiculously dry.

Such an interesting case study on what goes down when structural refining bottlenecks get hit with geopolitical event.

#oott #iran

Optical satellite prints from July 20th across major Middle East oil hubs.

I get it—the macro trajectory from here is all about inbound tanker flow, and loaded vessels clearing Hormuz without a hitch is anything but a given.

That said, screaming that "loadings are frozen and we're about to see massive production shut-ins" is way too early a call.

If we see these terminals sitting totally bone-dry for days straight, then the real pain hits the tape. Click to expand for higher resolution—I've circled the active tankers in red.

All imagery is stamped July 20th, save for UAE which is July 19th. Dropping the remaining prints in the thread.

#oott #iran
UAE+Kharg https://t.co/Q4w7Pp0OP5

Stripping out the macro overlay, here are a few charts that matter most for gold...

I'll admit the print isn't nearly as pristine as BTC or Pt right now—especially when you factor in MM positioning/ETF flow—but it’s looking pretty decent overall.

If you accept the thesis gold is a mandatory staple for portfolio allocation anyway, this might be a sweet spot to start quietly accumulating.
